title: Decision, Risk and AI
text:
New research platform


U.S. macro scenario monitoring with energy, geopolitics, and explicit lag logic


This page is the public-facing output of a daily static macro desk. It uses public market and macro series, an explicit scenario engine, and lagged event checks to show how a live shock propagates through rates, credit, inflation compensation, FX, and equities.


Snapshot JSONSignal dashboard CSVLag-check CSV




Decision, Risk and AI


Daily U.S. macro scenario monitor with global energy and geopolitical transmission


This page is rebuilt from public data and an explicit scenario model. It is designed to show how a desk economist could monitor a live macro shock, test whether market signals confirm the scenario, and keep the reasoning transparent instead of hiding it inside a black box.





Current read


De-escalation / relief


Oil risk premium fades, volatility falls, credit tightens back in, and risk assets recover as markets price less near-term disruption.


Generated: 2026-04-01 16:26 UTC


Active event lens: Middle East energy and shipping disruption since 2026-03-10


Runner-up scenario: Growth drag / trade shock at 25.6%.

Method


Signals are standardized across 1d, 2d, 5d, and 20d horizons, then matched against scenario sign expectations using explicit weights.


No black-box classifier is used in this version. The system is designed for economic interpretation first and machine assistance second.

Workflow


How this system is built every day



Pull official public series for energy, rates, inflation compensation, credit, FX, equities, and key macro releases.


Compute 1d, 2d, 5d, and 20d moves and standardize them against trailing history.


Score a small set of explicit scenarios using expected sign and signal importance.


Use the active event date to test whether lagged market behavior still confirms the chosen shock narrative.


Publish a static snapshot that is reproducible, reviewable, and easy to explain to a recruiter, hiring manager, or research lead.

title: Chapter 3
text:
Chapter 3 summary


When does extra information create enough decision improvement to be worth using?


This chapter treats information as an economic asset. The question is not whether a signal exists, but whether it changes action quality enough to matter under uncertainty.


Back to Research Atlas GitHub repo





Simple visual result


Paired-strip comparisons, treatment-effect summaries, and downside-risk views under signal and no-signal settings




Main contribution


Reframes precision-ag information as a decision asset whose value depends on changed action quality




Data


Paired-strip experimental data, monitored signals, treatment outcomes, and profitability measures




Main logic


Compare outcomes with and without the signal and identify whether the information changes decisions enough to create value






Methods


GAM-based regression, causal forests, quantile views, and treatment-effect interpretation under noisy field conditions.




Why it matters


The chapter shifts the discussion from descriptive information to usable information, which is the real decision problem.

title: Chapter 1
text:
Chapter 1 summary


When does information travel across fields, and when does it fail?


This chapter asks whether a model that performs well in one field can be trusted in a different field environment. The core issue is not prediction alone. The real issue is whether transferred information still supports a good decision.


Back to Research Atlas GitHub repo





Simple visual result


Holdout field maps, transfer-risk comparisons, and profit-loss contrasts across locations




Main contribution


Shows that predictive fit is not enough; structural validation is needed before transfer




Data


Field-level spatial data, management information, and response outcomes across multiple fields




Main logic


Train on one environment, hold out another, and measure whether decision quality survives transfer






Methods


XGBoost, Random Forest, holdout design, stability comparisons, and decision-loss interpretation under cross-field transfer.




Why it matters


A model can look strong in aggregate and still become risky when moved to a new spatial setting. This chapter makes that risk visible.

title: Portfolio
text:
Jaeseok Hwang




Research, code, and decision analytics



Ph.D. (Aug 2026) in Applied Economics, UIUC.


This page brings together my dissertation chapters and individual research portfolio, with a focus on producer risk management under price and environmental shocks. Methodologically, I work with supervised machine learning, including XGBoost and Random Forest, together with causal inference and spatial analytics.





GitHub LinkedIn




Project(Papers)




Chapter 1


Transferability of Multi-Field Information


Keywords: transferability, spatial heterogeneity, holdout validation


Open summary GitHub repo




Chapter 2


Information Validity and Profitability Risk


Keywords: profitability risk, exogenous shocks, Bayesian hierarchy


Open summary GitHub repo




Chapter 3


Value of Information in Stochastic Environments


Keywords: value of information, stochastic environments, causal inference


Open summary GitHub repo







New research


Decision, Risk and AI


Keywords: U.S. macro desk, public API pipeline, lagged scenario scoring


This project turns public macro and market data into a daily static scenario monitor for U.S. macro risk. The current build tracks energy and geopolitical shocks, measures transmission into rates, credit, FX, and equities, and publishes a recruiter-facing desk note every day.


The public page now functions as a live research surface rather than a placeholder. It shows the active event lens, scenario probabilities, lag confirmation table, and the daily market dashboard.


Open daily desk Website repo

title: Chapter 2
text:
Chapter 2 summary


Which part of weak profitability comes from the decision, and which part comes from outside shocks?


This chapter separates controllable decision error from uncontrollable weather and market shocks. The goal is to avoid confusing a bad decision rule with bad luck.


Back to Research Atlas GitHub repo





Simple visual result


Decomposition charts, realized-versus-optimal curves, and uncertainty bands around profit gaps




Main contribution


Separates decision-driven loss from exogenous shocks so outcome interpretation becomes more defensible




Data


Input decisions, response outcomes, and uncertainty from weather and price variation




Main logic


Compare realized outcomes against modeled benchmarks and assign the gap to controllable and uncontrollable components






Methods


Bayesian hierarchical modeling, nonlinear response structure, uncertainty decomposition, and profitability-risk interpretation.




Why it matters


This chapter gives economic meaning to model evaluation by showing whether the system failed or whether the environment moved against the decision.

title: Research Atlas
text:
Three dissertation chapters


Simple chapter shortcuts


This page stays intentionally short. Each chapter card shows the topic, the main focus, and the repository link. The chapter page holds the concise summary with visual result, contribution, data, logic, and method.


Open Spatial Decision Lab GitHub Profile





Chapter 1


Transferability of Multi-Field Information


Main focus: whether a model trained in one field can be trusted in a structurally different field.


Open chapter page GitHub repo




Chapter 2


Information Validity and Profitability Risk


Main focus: which part of weak profitability comes from the decision and which part comes from external shocks.


Open chapter page GitHub repo




Chapter 3


Value of Information in Stochastic Environments


Main focus: when monitored signals change decisions enough to create real economic value.


Open chapter page GitHub repo
